source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — GPUs, TPUs, economics of AI (Nvidia vs Google)
summary: AI ROI is empirically positive and unambiguous based on ROIC data from public GPU spenders
summary_extended: Baker argues that the ROI debate around AI spending is empirically settled and 'strange' given publicly available data: the largest GPU buyers are public companies with audited financials, and their ROIC is measurably higher now than before they ramped GPU capex. He identifies two primary drivers of this ROIC improvement — opex savings from AI automation and the migration of recommendation and advertising systems from CPUs to GPUs, which has driven both efficiency gains and revenue acceleration. As a cautionary counterpoint, Baker notes that Meta's ROIC declined in the one quarter it failed to produce a frontier model, underscoring that continued AI investment and capability advancement is necessary to sustain these returns. The investment implication is that the largest hyperscaler GPU spenders — companies with the scale to deploy AI across advertising, cloud, and consumer products — are generating real, auditable returns, making the bear case on AI capex ROI empirically weak. Baker frames this as a structural, ongoing dynamic rather than a one-time event, suggesting continued GPU spending by these companies is justified and likely.
detail: The largest GPU buyers are public companies with audited financials. Their ROIC is higher now than before they ramped GPU spending. Key drivers: opex savings, and moving recommendation/advertising systems from CPUs to GPUs driving massive efficiency gains and revenue acceleration. Meta's ROIC declined in one quarter when it couldn't make a frontier model—a cautionary data point.
confidence_evidence: Baker calls the ROI debate 'strange' given publicly available ROIC data and frames it as 'empirically, factually, unambiguously positive'
quote_fragment: the ROIC of the big public spenders on GPUs is higher than before they ramped spending

ticker_audit.reasoning: Meta is explicitly named as the primary case study — Baker cites Meta's ROIC improvement from GPU spending on recommendation/advertising systems and specifically calls out the one quarter where Meta's ROIC declined when it failed to produce a frontier model.

Google is one of the largest public GPU spenders with audited financials and benefits directly from the thesis — its advertising and cloud businesses are prime examples of the CPU-to-GPU migration Baker describes driving ROIC improvement.

Amazon is a major public GPU spender whose AWS cloud and advertising businesses fit Baker's framework of AI-driven ROIC improvement; its financials are auditable and it is among the companies Baker's thesis implicitly covers.
